skill.name: project-development
skill.description: Plan and structure LLM-powered projects with pipeline architecture, task-model fit checks, cost estimates, and agent-assisted development patterns.
skill.summary: Design practical LLM project pipelines before committing to automation.
skill.author: muratcankoylan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found the command, URL, and weak-crypto matches are mostly documentation or code-example false positives, but the included Python pipeline template performs local file writes and deletes based on unsanitized identifiers. The skill is publishable with a warning because the risky behavior is a customizable template, not confirmed malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes And Deletes Use Unvalidated Identifiers
medium_findings.description: The Python template derives data and output paths from batch_id and item identifiers, then writes pipeline artifacts and deletes stage outputs. If reused unchanged with untrusted identifiers, path traversal can move writes or deletes outside the intended data and output directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and confirmed in the executable template. Impact depends on whether users customize the template safely and whether batch or item identifiers are trusted.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Content Is Inserted Directly Into LLM Prompts
medium_findings.description: The template formats title and content directly into the prompt used for the LLM call. Source content is not isolated or labeled as untrusted data, so hostile input can influence model output and downstream parsing if the template processes adversarial content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompt interpolation path is clear, but the current template uses mock data and a mock LLM response. The risk becomes material when adapted to untrusted external inputs.

low_findings.title: Static Command Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick command matches occur in markdown code fences, pipeline diagrams, and prose examples. I did not find evidence that these markdown snippets execute commands during skill activation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples or diagrams, not executable Ruby or shell code. No runtime command execution path was found in those files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to public project and article references used as citations. I did not find evidence that the skill fetches these URLs or sends data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are external resource citations in markdown. There is no corresponding network call in the executable template.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Non-Cryptographic Text
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography detections map to prose, model names, regular expressions, or CLI help text rather than cryptographic APIs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak algorithms being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context does not contain cryptographic operations. Confidence is high, though the static result list was noisy and not every duplicate line was individually restated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path-Derived Filesystem Mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: The template combines user-supplied or source-derived identifiers with base directories before creating, writing, and deleting files. Add identifier validation and path containment checks before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in executable Python code. Exploitability depends on input trust boundaries in the user adaptation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Prompt Interpolation From Source Content
dangerous_patterns.description: The template inserts source title and content into an LLM prompt without strong data boundaries. Add untrusted-content framing and output validation for hostile inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from source item fields to prompt text is explicit. The current mock implementation limits immediate impact.

content.value_statement: LLM projects often fail when teams automate before proving task fit and cost. This skill provides a practical planning method, pipeline structure, and reusable template for staged development.
actual_capabilities: Evaluates whether a task fits LLM strengths before automation begins.
Defines an acquire, prepare, process, parse, and render pipeline model.
Explains filesystem-based state, caching, retry, and debugging patterns.
Includes a Python batch pipeline template with CLI stages and mock processing.
Provides case studies for batch analysis, tool reduction, and multi-agent research.
Guides cost estimation using item counts, token estimates, and retry buffers.
limitations: The Python template uses mock data and mock LLM output until customized.
It does not provide production API integrations, authentication, or deployment code.
Filesystem path safety and sandboxing must be added before processing untrusted identifiers.
Pricing examples and model names should be checked before budget decisions.

use_cases.title: Plan A Batch Analysis Pipeline
use_cases.description: Use the staged pipeline model to process many independent items with cached prompts, responses, parsed results, and final reports.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Task-Model Fit
use_cases.description: Decide whether an LLM approach is suitable by testing representative inputs, acceptable error rates, and expected output quality.

use_cases.title: Estimate LLM Project Cost
use_cases.description: Build an early cost model from item volume, token estimates, model pricing, retries, and expected development work.

prompt_templates.title: Check Task Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a possible LLM feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this task is a good fit for LLM processing: [describe task]. Consider input type, output type, error tolerance, determinism, latency, and cost.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Pipeline Stages
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an acquire, prepare, process, parse, and render pipeline for this project: [project details]. Include stage inputs, outputs, cached files, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Create Cost Estimate
prompt_templates.prompt: Estimate the cost for this LLM batch job: [item count], [average input size], [expected output size], [model], and [retry rate]. Show assumptions and risk areas.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this LLM pipeline design for production readiness: [design]. Focus on path safety, prompt injection, retries, observability, parsing failures, rate limits, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: A team wants to analyze 20,000 support tickets with summaries and severity labels.
output: Task fit assessment with expected strengths and failure modes.
Five-stage pipeline outline with cached intermediate artifacts.
Cost estimate inputs, retry buffer, and validation checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to structure a prototype for an LLM report generator.
output: Manual validation plan for one representative example.
Suggested files for raw data, prompts, responses, parsed results, and rendered reports.
Iteration plan for parsing, evaluation, and prompt refinement.

output_examples.input: An engineering lead asks whether to use one agent or multiple agents for research synthesis.
output: Recommendation based on context size, task value, and independence of subtasks.
Cost tradeoff summary for single-agent and multi-agent approaches.
Evaluation plan using representative queries and human review.

best_practices: Validate one representative example manually before building automation.
Keep pipeline stages discrete, idempotent, and easy to rerun.
Track token usage, parsing failures, and output quality from the first prototype.
anti_patterns: Building a full pipeline before proving that the model can solve the task.
Combining acquisition, LLM calls, parsing, and rendering in one opaque script.
Trusting LLM output formats without tolerant parsing and validation.

faq.question: Does this skill build a complete production system?
faq.answer: No. It provides methodology, planning guidance, and a customizable template for early pipeline development.

faq.question: Can it help choose between LLMs and traditional code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes task-fit criteria for deciding when LLM processing is useful or inappropriate.

faq.question: Does the included script call an LLM API?
faq.answer: No. The template uses a mock response and comments showing where a real API call can be added.

faq.question: Is it useful for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-agnostic and applies to agent-assisted development in all three environments.

faq.question: What security precautions are needed?
faq.answer: Validate path inputs, sandbox file operations, treat source content as untrusted, and verify parsed outputs before use.

faq.question: When should teams avoid this approach?
faq.answer: Avoid it for tasks requiring perfect accuracy, deterministic computation, low latency, or strict real-time behavior.
